title: Test Container Lifecycle Management

start_flow: START: start() called by Quarkus test framework
WAIT: Wait.forLogMessage('.*database system is ready to accept connections.*', 2) — blocks until ready (timeout: 30s)
RETURN: Map<String, String> config params to Quarkus test framework
stop_flow: START: stop() called by Quarkus test framework
container_init: STATIC INIT: GenericContainer created with image POSTGRES_IMAGE

start_flow: START: start() called by Quarkus test framework
→ Starts KafkaContainer and SchemaRegistryContainer in dependency order (Schema depends on Kafka)
→ Blocks until both containers are fully started (SchemaRegistry timeout: 90s)
RETURN: Map<String, String> config params to Quarkus test framework
stop_flow: START: stop() called by Quarkus test framework
→ NOTE: kafkaContainer is NOT explicitly stopped here
container_init: STATIC INIT: Network.newNetwork() — shared Docker network
STATIC INIT: KafkaContainer created with image quay.io/debezium/confluentinc-cp-kafka:7.2.10

key_relationships: KafkaContainer and SchemaRegistryContainer share a Docker Network
Startables.deepStart() manages ordered parallel startup of Kafka + SchemaRegistry
GenericContainer (Postgres) is a static singleton — shared across test instances
important_notes: Both managers implement QuarkusTestResourceLifecycleManager — lifecycle driven by Quarkus test framework
All containers are static fields — single instance per JVM, shared across test classes
Postgres uses simple container.start(); Schema Registry uses Startables.deepStart() for dependency ordering
SchemaRegistry stop does NOT explicitly stop KafkaContainer — potential resource leak
Postgres wait strategy: log pattern match (2 occurrences), 30s timeout
SchemaRegistry startup timeout: 90s (longer due to Kafka + registry startup chain)
Port 5432 exposed for Postgres; port 8081 for Schema Registry
